title: Berry Curvature Two-Form
summary: The Berry curvature two-form F is the gauge-invariant curvature of the Berry connection and integrates to the first Chern number on closed surfaces.
assumptions: The relevant band bundle is smooth on the domain.
regime: Regular momentum-space region or parameter-space surface.
scope: Lecture One curvature two-form of the occupied-state line bundle.

source_anchors.notes: Lecture One identifies F/2pi with the first Chern class and notes dF=0 away from singularities.

representation: Gauge-invariant curvature two-form associated with Berry transport.
motivation: This note exists because once the Berry connection is introduced, the next reusable mathematical object is its curvature two-form F. This is the quantity whose surface integral is topological and whose closedness drives the Stokes arguments in Lecture One.
prerequisite_story: Know the Berry connection A on the occupied-state line bundle and the abelian rule F = dA. The domain must stay inside a smooth region of the filled-state bundle so that F is an honest differential two-form there.
local_context_story: In Witten Lecture One, F is the explicit object whose integral over a sphere around a bad point gives the local node charge. It is also the representative of the first Chern class that turns bundle topology into a calculable surface integral.
forward_links_story: This quantity feeds directly into the local charge formula w_alpha = integral F/2pi, the punctured-zone dF = 0 argument, and later the two-dimensional TKNN invariant over the Brillouin torus.
canonical_questions: Why is F the gauge-invariant object while A is gauge dependent?
Why does integrating F over a closed surface produce an integer Chern number?
What is the precise meaning of dF = 0 away from singularities?
How is this same two-form reused in Lecture Two for the TKNN invariant?
scope_boundaries: The two-form is regular only on the smooth domain of the occupied bundle; singular bad points are excluded from the local differential-geometric treatment.
In a higher-rank occupied bundle, one keeps track of a matrix-valued non-abelian curvature before forming gauge-invariant traced quantities.

math_expressions.notes: Definition of the Berry-curvature two-form in the abelian rank-one case.
The integral over a closed surface Sigma measures the first Chern number of the occupied-state line bundle restricted to Sigma.
Closedness of the curvature away from singular sources.

symbols.meaning: closed oriented surface in parameter or momentum space

failure_modes: At singular points the form is not globally regular.
retrieval_hints: Use when a query asks for the geometric meaning of Berry curvature.
